item.title: GAEC Africa Archivist-in-Residence 2026 in Senegal: Apply to Build a Decolonial Digital Archive in Saint-Louis
item.dc:creator: Muhammad Chishti
item.description:
About the GAEC Africa Archivist-in-Residence Opportunity The GAEC Africa Archivist-in-Residence programme has been established to document, organize, interpret, and digitally preserve materials connected to Groundings, a decolonial convening held in 2025. According to the opportunity listing, the selected Archivist-in-Residence will: Spend one month residing in Saint-Louis, Senegal; Work from GAEC-Africa’s community space; Develop a digital […]

The post GAEC Africa Archivist-in-Residence 2026 in Senegal: Apply to Build a Decolonial Digital Archive in Saint-Louis appeared first on Opportunities for Youth.
item.content:encoded:
About the GAEC Africa Archivist-in-Residence Opportunity

The GAEC Africa Archivist-in-Residence programme has been established to document, organize, interpret, and digitally preserve materials connected to Groundings, a decolonial convening held in 2025.

According to the opportunity listing, the selected Archivist-in-Residence will:

  • Spend one month residing in Saint-Louis, Senegal;
  • Work from GAEC-Africa’s community space;
  • Develop a digital archive of the 2025 Groundings convening;
  • Produce an archive that is original, engaging, and rich in source material; and
  • Engage meaningfully with GAEC-Africa’s student-activist community.

This is described as GAEC-Africa’s inaugural Archivist-in-Residence opportunity, meaning that the selected applicant will help shape not only the archive itself but potentially the working approach for future archival residencies and documentation initiatives.

What Is Groundings?

Groundings is identified in the call as GAEC-Africa’s 2025 decolonial convening. Although the detailed programme of the 2025 gathering is not included in the publicly accessible Rhizome summary, the wider intellectual context of GAEC-Africa’s Groundings work is rooted in decolonial thought, Black radical traditions, political economy, community learning, and the relationship between locally situated knowledge and global structures.

GAEC-Africa’s seminars and community projects have previously created spaces in which students, scholars, activists, and community members examine issues including postcolonial societies, capitalism, resistance, gender, labour, imperialism, and global Blackness. These activities seek to connect critical theory with the social, cultural, and political experiences of communities in and around Saint-Louis.

The proposed digital archive will therefore be more than a conventional collection of files. It is expected to preserve the intellectual exchanges, community participation, creative practices, discussions, and forms of knowledge production that emerged from the convening.

About GAEC-Africa

The Group of Action and Critical Study-Africa, commonly known as GAEC-Africa, is an intellectual, artistic, and community-based ecosystem founded in 2020. It brings together researchers, artists, students, activists, professionals, volunteers, community members, and critical thinkers to co-produce knowledge from Africa and the Global South.

GAEC-Africa describes itself through four interconnected identities:

  1. An association of academics;
  2. A critical study group;
  3. An African initiative; and
  4. A decolonial collective.

Its work is concerned with historical, cultural, political, epistemic, economic, racial, and social injustices. It promotes research combined with action through seminars, community workshops, advocacy, public awareness initiatives, experiential learning, curriculum development, cultural programming, and community engagement.

GAEC-Africa’s Three Main Areas of Work

GAEC-Africa organizes its activities around three principal ecosystems.

1. Centre Universitaire Panafricain et Vernaculaire

This platform concentrates on research, popular and critical knowledge, counter-archives, seminars, publications, podcasts, fellowships, and experimental approaches to knowledge production.

Its programmes seek to provide learning opportunities grounded in critical theory, Pan-Africanism, decolonial thought, and the Black radical tradition. The seminars are intended to connect academic inquiry with community experiences and create spaces where knowledge can be produced collectively rather than transferred only through conventional institutions.

2. Ndar Demb ak Tey

Ndar Demb ak Tey focuses on residencies, cultural production, exhibitions, performances, curatorial initiatives, festivals, and community events.

Its research and archiving mission seeks to reinterpret the history of Saint-Louis through oral histories, objects, memories, struggles, experiences, and accounts contributed by the city’s inhabitants. GAEC-Africa describes this work as both a dynamic oral-history museum project and a pedagogical programme concerned with the contemporary experiences of societies in the Global South.

The Archivist-in-Residence opportunity is strongly aligned with this institutional commitment to community-based archiving and the preservation of situated knowledge.

3. Black Teraanga Bloc

The Black Teraanga Bloc supports public interventions, citizen initiatives, campaigns, community mobilization, dialogues, and actions focused on hospitality, solidarity, social justice, and public engagement.

Together, these three areas connect research, art, archiving, education, activism, cultural practice, and community participation.

What Will the Archivist-in-Residence Do?

The central responsibility will be to transform the documentation of the Groundings convening into a coherent and accessible digital archive.

The listing does not provide a detailed technical terms of reference in its publicly accessible summary. However, producing the type of digital archive described in the call would ordinarily involve several interconnected stages.

1. Review and Inventory Available Materials

The resident may need to review the materials produced before, during, and after Groundings. Depending on what GAEC-Africa has collected, these could include:

  • Photographs;
  • Video recordings;
  • Audio recordings;
  • Event programmes;
  • Presentation materials;
  • Participant notes;
  • Transcripts;
  • Posters and visual designs;
  • Interviews;
  • Written reflections;
  • Biographical information;
  • Reading lists;
  • Workshop outputs;
  • Correspondence;
  • Community testimonies; and
  • Other documentary or artistic materials.

These examples illustrate the types of content that may form part of a convening archive; they should not be treated as a confirmed inventory of the materials available.

2. Develop an Archival Structure

The Archivist-in-Residence may be expected to determine how the material should be classified, described, connected, and presented.

This could involve developing:

  • A clear archival hierarchy;
  • Categories and subcategories;
  • A file-naming system;
  • Descriptive metadata;
  • Contributor information;
  • Chronological or thematic groupings;
  • Searchable keywords;
  • Contextual descriptions;
  • Cross-references between related materials; and
  • Guidelines for the future addition of content.

A strong digital archive should enable users to understand not only what an item is but also who produced it, why it matters, how it relates to the convening, and what historical or intellectual context surrounds it.

3. Digitally Curate the Groundings Story

The call asks for a digital archive that is dynamic, original, and material-packed. This wording suggests that GAEC-Africa is seeking more than a basic online storage folder or document repository.

The resident may need to consider how different forms of material can be combined to communicate the history and significance of Groundings. This could include thematic collections, multimedia narratives, participant profiles, timelines, annotated galleries, interactive pathways, or other approaches to digital storytelling.

The final structure should preserve the complexity of the convening without removing the voices, context, and relationships that gave the materials their meaning.

4. Engage the Student-Activist Community

Community engagement is an expressly stated component of the residency. The successful applicant will work closely with GAEC-Africa’s local student-activist community rather than developing the archive in isolation.

This engagement may help the resident:

  • Understand the history and purpose of the convening;
  • Identify important themes and moments;
  • Obtain context for specific materials;
  • Clarify names, dates, locations, and events;
  • Understand how participants want their contributions represented;
  • Collect additional reflections or descriptions;
  • Build local archival and digital-documentation capacity; and
  • Ensure that the archive reflects community perspectives.

A participatory process is especially important for a decolonial archive. Decisions regarding classification, description, authorship, access, and interpretation can determine whose knowledge is made visible and whose voice is marginalized.

Why This Digital Archive Matters

Archives are not neutral collections. The decisions made about what is preserved, how it is categorized, who is credited, and how material is accessed influence how future users understand an event, institution, or community.

The GAEC Africa Archivist-in-Residence project has the potential to contribute in several important ways.

Preservation of Community Knowledge

Events such as Groundings may generate valuable discussions, creative materials, recordings, and collective reflections. Without deliberate preservation, these materials can become fragmented, inaccessible, or lost.

A structured digital archive can protect this knowledge and make it available for future research, education, artistic practice, community organizing, and institutional learning.

Development of a Counter-Archive

GAEC-Africa’s institutional work includes the production of critical knowledge, popular knowledge, and counter-archives. A counter-archive can challenge dominant historical narratives by preserving materials, perspectives, and lived experiences that have often been excluded from conventional institutions.

The Groundings archive may therefore help document intellectual and political work from Saint-Louis on its own terms rather than filtering it exclusively through external academic or cultural frameworks.

Connection Between Local and Global Conversations

GAEC-Africa’s work is locally situated in Senegal while remaining connected to African, diasporic, and Global South intellectual networks.

A well-developed online archive could allow students, researchers, artists, activists, and community organizations outside Saint-Louis to engage with the ideas and materials generated through Groundings.

Institutional Learning

The residency can also help GAEC-Africa strengthen its internal systems for documentation, digital preservation, metadata management, consent, access, and long-term archival maintenance.

Because this is the inaugural Archivist-in-Residence programme, the resident’s methods and recommendations could potentially inform how the organization manages future events and collections.

Who Is the Opportunity Best Suited For?

The publicly accessible summary does not prescribe a particular academic degree, professional certification, age range, or minimum number of years of experience.

Based on the nature of the assignment, it appears particularly relevant to:

  • Archivists and digital archivists;
  • Librarians and information-management professionals;
  • Digital-humanities researchers;
  • Public historians;
  • Curators and cultural heritage professionals;
  • Documentary artists;
  • Multimedia storytellers;
  • Digital artists and creative technologists;
  • Researchers working on African, Black, or decolonial studies;
  • Oral-history practitioners;
  • Museum and collection professionals;
  • Web-based exhibition designers;
  • Community archive practitioners; and
  • Individuals experienced in participatory documentation.

Applicants do not necessarily need to fit only one professional category. The residency appears interdisciplinary and could suit someone who combines archival practice with research, artistic production, digital design, documentary work, or community engagement.

Relevant Skills for the Residency

A competitive applicant would likely be able to demonstrate several of the following capabilities:

Archival and Information-Management Skills

  • Organizing physical and digital records;
  • Designing archive structures;
  • Cataloguing and classification;
  • Creating metadata;
  • Developing naming conventions and taxonomies;
  • Managing multiple file formats;
  • Applying digital-preservation practices; and
  • Documenting provenance, ownership, and context.

Digital and Multimedia Skills

  • Developing digital exhibitions or online archives;
  • Working with content-management systems;
  • Editing photographs, audio, or video;
  • Digitizing documents and media;
  • Managing large digital collections;
  • Designing accessible user experiences;
  • Building interactive timelines or multimedia narratives; and
  • Preparing web-ready archival content.

Research and Writing Skills

  • Conducting contextual and historical research;
  • Writing accurate descriptions;
  • Summarizing complex discussions;
  • Interviewing participants;
  • Fact-checking names, dates, and events;
  • Developing interpretive texts; and
  • Presenting academic or community knowledge in an accessible form.

Community-Engagement Skills

  • Working respectfully with students, activists, artists, and local communities;
  • Facilitating collaborative decisions;
  • Handling sensitive or personal information ethically;
  • Obtaining and documenting consent;
  • Recognizing community ownership of knowledge;
  • Listening to different perspectives; and
  • Avoiding extractive approaches to documentation.

Understanding of Decolonial Archival Practice

Because the archive concerns a decolonial convening and will be developed within an organization explicitly committed to decolonial knowledge production, applicants should understand that archival categories and institutional practices can reproduce unequal power relationships.

Experience or demonstrated interest in African studies, Black studies, colonial history, oral history, community archives, epistemic justice, or related areas would therefore be valuable.

Residency Location and Duration

The selected Archivist-in-Residence will spend one month in Saint-Louis, Senegal, and work from GAEC-Africa’s community space.

Saint-Louis provides an important setting for the assignment because GAEC-Africa’s archiving work is closely connected to the histories, memories, communities, and contemporary experiences of the city. The organization is based in Saint-Louis and maintains active relationships with students, researchers, artists, activists, residents, and international academic partners.

Applicants should be prepared for an immersive, community-based residency rather than a fully remote archival assignment.

Application Preparation

The publicly accessible listing does not identify the complete list of required application documents. Applicants should rely on the official application instructions linked to the opportunity before submitting.

To prepare a strong application, candidates may find it useful to assemble:

  1. An updated curriculum vitae;
  2. A concise statement explaining their interest in the residency;
  3. A portfolio of digital archives, exhibitions, websites, publications, or documentary projects;
  4. Examples of archival organization, metadata, cataloguing, or digital storytelling;
  5. A proposed approach to documenting Groundings;
  6. Evidence of experience working with communities;
  7. An explanation of their interest in decolonial or community-centred archival practice;
  8. Information about relevant software and technical skills;
  9. Details of language competencies; and
  10. Confirmation of availability to reside in Saint-Louis for one month.

These are preparation recommendations and should not be interpreted as a confirmed list of mandatory documents.

Important Questions Applicants Should Confirm

The accessible public listing does not provide complete information about the financial and logistical terms of the residency. Before accepting an offer, shortlisted applicants should obtain clear information about:

  • Whether an honorarium or professional fee will be provided;
  • Whether international or domestic travel will be covered;
  • Whether accommodation is supplied;
  • Whether meals or a daily allowance are included;
  • Visa and immigration support;
  • Health or travel insurance;
  • Local transportation;
  • Workspace and equipment;
  • Internet and software access;
  • Required working languages;
  • Ownership and copyright of the final archive;
  • Consent and data-protection arrangements;
  • Public access to sensitive materials;
  • Credit and attribution;
  • Technical hosting of the archive; and
  • Responsibility for maintaining the archive after the residency.

Applicants should not assume that any of these costs or services are covered unless the organizer confirms them.

Application Deadline

The deadline stated in the Rhizome opportunity listing is:

August 3, 2026, at 12:00 p.m.

The time zone is not displayed in the indexed listing. Applicants should therefore avoid waiting until the final day and should consult the official application materials for the exact closing time. Click HERE to apply.

Conclusion

The GAEC Africa Archivist-in-Residence 2026 offers a distinctive opportunity to combine digital archiving, decolonial knowledge production, community participation, and creative storytelling.

The selected resident will spend one month in Saint-Louis developing a digital archive of Groundings, GAEC-Africa’s 2025 decolonial convening. The assignment will require more than technical file management. It calls for an applicant capable of understanding the intellectual and community context of the materials, working collaboratively with the local student-activist community, and translating a complex convening into an accessible and meaningful digital resource.

For archivists, researchers, curators, digital-humanities practitioners, documentary artists, and community historians interested in African and decolonial knowledge systems, this residency presents an opportunity to contribute to the preservation of an important intellectual and cultural initiative while helping establish a long-term archival foundation for GAEC-Africa.

For more global opportunities, VISIT HERE.

The post GAEC Africa Archivist-in-Residence 2026 in Senegal: Apply to Build a Decolonial Digital Archive in Saint-Louis appeared first on Opportunities for Youth.

item.title: African Union–UNDP YouthConnekt Africa Export Accelerator 2026: Applications Open for Youth-Led Businesses Expanding Across Africa
item.dc:creator: Muhammad Chishti
item.description:
Application Deadline: 16 August 2026 Applications are open for the YouthConnekt Africa Export Accelerator 2026, a specialised business growth and export-readiness programme supporting young African entrepreneurs who have already established businesses and are prepared to expand beyond their domestic markets. The initiative, also known as the E-Accelerator, was developed through a partnership involving YouthConnekt Africa, […]

The post African Union–UNDP YouthConnekt Africa Export Accelerator 2026: Applications Open for Youth-Led Businesses Expanding Across Africa appeared first on Opportunities for Youth.
item.content:encoded:
Application Deadline: 16 August 2026

Applications are open for the YouthConnekt Africa Export Accelerator 2026, a specialised business growth and export-readiness programme supporting young African entrepreneurs who have already established businesses and are prepared to expand beyond their domestic markets.

The initiative, also known as the E-Accelerator, was developed through a partnership involving YouthConnekt Africa, the United Nations Development Programme and the African Union. It is designed to help export-ready youth-led enterprises overcome the regulatory, operational and commercial barriers that often prevent promising African businesses from entering new markets within the continent.

The programme’s pilot phase is open to eligible entrepreneurs from Rwanda, Uganda, Senegal, The Gambia, Zambia and Zimbabwe. Applicants must be below 35 years of age, own or operate an established business and demonstrate that their enterprise is ready to explore regional expansion.

The E-Accelerator is not intended for entrepreneurs who are only searching for grants. Instead, it targets ambitious and growth-oriented business owners who are willing to strengthen their enterprises, improve their export capacity and become competitive participants in intra-African trade.

About the YouthConnekt Africa Export Accelerator 2026

The YouthConnekt Africa Export Accelerator is a results-driven initiative created to support youth-led enterprises seeking to scale into new African markets.

Although many young entrepreneurs build successful businesses within their home countries, expanding into another African market can present considerable challenges. Businesses may encounter complex customs processes, different product standards, regulatory compliance requirements, limited information about foreign markets, difficulties finding distributors and inadequate access to growth financing.

The E-Accelerator seeks to address these challenges through a structured process combining:

  • Business diagnostics;
  • Export-readiness assessment;
  • Tailored enterprise advisory;
  • Business strengthening;
  • Export accreditation;
  • Policy and regulatory support;
  • Market-access facilitation;
  • Exposure to business opportunities; and
  • Connections to financing and investment opportunities.

The programme goes beyond conventional entrepreneurship training. Its purpose is not simply to organise workshops or provide general business lessons. It aims to understand the specific circumstances of each participating enterprise and offer support relevant to its export ambitions, readiness level and growth barriers.

Connection to the African Continental Free Trade Area

The YouthConnekt Africa Export Accelerator operates within the framework of the African Continental Free Trade Area, commonly known as the AfCFTA.

The AfCFTA is intended to increase trade among African countries by improving market access, reducing trade barriers and strengthening regional economic integration. However, many youth-led enterprises remain unable to benefit fully from this continental market because of regulatory, financial and operational constraints.

The E-Accelerator is particularly aligned with the AfCFTA Protocol on Women and Youth in Trade, which seeks to improve the participation of young people and women in regional commerce.

By helping businesses prepare for export, comply with market requirements and address non-tariff barriers, the programme aims to increase the number of youth-led enterprises capable of participating meaningfully in intra-African trade.

Main Objectives of the Programme

The YouthConnekt Africa Export Accelerator seeks to help participating enterprises:

  1. Evaluate their current level of export readiness.
  2. Identify weaknesses in their business and operational systems.
  3. Strengthen their capacity to enter new African markets.
  4. Understand customs, certification and regulatory requirements.
  5. Improve their competitiveness in regional markets.
  6. Develop realistic market-expansion strategies.
  7. Address non-tariff barriers affecting cross-border trade.
  8. Improve access to finance and investment opportunities.
  9. Establish relationships with regional business partners.
  10. Participate more effectively in intra-African trade.
  11. Expand their customer and market base.
  12. Mobilise investment for business growth.
  13. Create productive and sustainable employment opportunities.
  14. Contribute to stronger regional value chains across Africa.

The programme is therefore intended to convert promising locally established businesses into more competitive, export-capable and regionally connected enterprises.

Countries Participating in the Pilot Phase

The first phase of the E-Accelerator is being implemented in six African countries:

  • Rwanda;
  • Uganda;
  • Senegal;
  • The Gambia;
  • Zambia; and
  • Zimbabwe.

Applicants must be operating in one of these countries to qualify for the 2026 pilot cohort.

The current call is not presented as being open to entrepreneurs from every African country. Entrepreneurs based outside the six participating countries should therefore not assume that they are eligible for the pilot phase.

Who Can Apply?

The programme is designed for young entrepreneurs who meet the following conditions:

Applicants Must Be Below 35 Years of Age

Applicants must be under 35 years old.

The published eligibility condition specifically refers to entrepreneurs below the age of 35. Applicants should ensure that they meet this requirement at the time of applying.

Applicants Must Be From an Eligible Country

Applicants must be from or operating their businesses in one of the following countries:

  • Rwanda;
  • Uganda;
  • Senegal;
  • The Gambia;
  • Zambia; or
  • Zimbabwe.

Applicants Must Own or Operate an Established Business

The applicant must own, lead or operate an enterprise that is already established at the local level.

This is not primarily an idea-stage entrepreneurship programme. Applicants should already have a functioning business rather than only a proposed concept that has not commenced operations.

The Business Must Be Ready for Expansion

The enterprise should have reached a stage where it is prepared to explore markets beyond its home country.

Applicants should be able to explain:

  • What their business currently does;
  • Which products or services it offers;
  • Who its customers are;
  • What progress it has achieved locally;
  • Why its offering is suitable for other African markets;
  • Which markets it would like to enter;
  • What is preventing the business from expanding; and
  • What support it requires to become export-ready.

Applicants Must Be Interested in Intra-African Trade

The entrepreneur should be committed to developing a competitive business capable of trading across African borders.

The programme is best suited to applicants who are willing to strengthen their internal business systems, address regulatory requirements and develop a realistic market-expansion strategy.

Important Notice: This Is Not a Grant-Only Programme

The programme organisers clearly state that the E-Accelerator is not intended for applicants who are only interested in receiving grants.

The opportunity focuses on:

  • Business development;
  • Export preparation;
  • Enterprise diagnostics;
  • Technical advisory;
  • Regulatory compliance;
  • Market access;
  • Export accreditation;
  • Investment readiness; and
  • Regional business expansion.

Applicants should not assume that participation includes an automatic cash award, startup grant, stipend or guaranteed investment.

Although participants may receive exposure to financing opportunities, this does not mean that every selected business will receive funding. Any future financing would likely depend on the enterprise’s performance, readiness, financial requirements and the criteria of the relevant investor or financial institution.

Entrepreneurs should therefore apply because they genuinely want to expand their businesses and become more competitive in African markets, not solely because they expect direct financial assistance.

What the E-Accelerator Offers

The programme includes an integrated journey intended to guide participating businesses from initial assessment to export preparation and market exposure.

  1. Application and Business Diagnostics

The first stage involves completing the Youth-Led Enterprise Registration and Diagnostic Form.

The form collects detailed information about the entrepreneur, business operations, growth plans, current challenges and export ambitions.

The diagnostic stage helps the programme organisers understand:

  • The current condition of the enterprise;
  • Its business model;
  • Its operational capacity;
  • Its readiness to expand;
  • Its priority markets;
  • Its regulatory challenges;
  • Its financing requirements; and
  • The type of advisory support it may require.

The application form may appear lengthy because it is intended to capture sufficient information for a meaningful diagnostic assessment.

Applicants are encouraged to provide both compulsory and optional information. Complete and accurate responses can help programme organisers design more relevant and individualised advisory support.

Information submitted may also be used to inform the diagnostic process and facilitate participation in related business opportunities.

  1. Selection and Announcement of the Pilot Cohort

Following the application and diagnostic process, submissions will be reviewed and a pilot group of qualifying enterprises will be selected.

The publicly available programme information does not specify the number of enterprises that will be chosen.

Selection is expected to focus on applicants who:

  • Meet the age and country requirements;
  • Operate an established business;
  • Demonstrate realistic growth potential;
  • Show readiness for regional expansion;
  • Clearly identify their export challenges; and
  • Are committed to participating in the complete accelerator journey.

Applicants should therefore provide evidence-based responses rather than broad or unsupported claims.

  1. Export FitLab

Selected enterprises will participate in an Export FitLab.

This component is intended to examine whether the enterprise is adequately prepared to enter another African market.

The Export FitLab may help participants evaluate:

  • The export suitability of their products or services;
  • Their production or service-delivery capacity;
  • Their financial readiness;
  • Their target-market selection;
  • Their pricing structure;
  • Their internal management systems;
  • Their compliance status;
  • Their distribution strategy; and
  • Their ability to manage regional growth.

The assessment should help participants understand the difference between simply wanting to export and being operationally prepared to do so.

  1. Tailored Enterprise Advisory

Participating businesses will receive advisory support based on their individual circumstances.

Different enterprises face different barriers. For example, a food-processing company may require assistance with product certification and packaging standards, while a digital-services company may need guidance on taxation, licensing, payment systems or intellectual-property protection.

Advisory support may address areas such as:

  • Business strategy;
  • Export planning;
  • Market research;
  • Financial management;
  • Operations;
  • Product development;
  • Certification;
  • Regulatory compliance;
  • Production capacity;
  • Pricing;
  • Logistics;
  • Distribution;
  • Access to finance;
  • Investment readiness; and
  • Regional partnership development.

The tailored model ensures that businesses are not given identical advice regardless of their sector, size or export-readiness level.

  1. Continental Bootcamp

The E-Accelerator journey includes a Continental Bootcamp during the YouthConnekt Africa Summit.

The bootcamp is expected to provide participating entrepreneurs with an opportunity to:

  • Strengthen their export knowledge;
  • Engage with other African entrepreneurs;
  • Learn from experts and business leaders;
  • Exchange market insights;
  • Build professional networks;
  • Meet potential regional partners;
  • Understand continental trade opportunities; and
  • Increase the visibility of their enterprises.

The public information reviewed does not confirm whether participants’ travel, accommodation, visa or related expenses for the bootcamp will be covered. Applicants should wait for direct confirmation from the organisers rather than assuming that all participation costs will be funded.

  1. Business Export Accreditation

The programme includes a Business Export Accreditation stage.

This component is intended to help participating enterprises demonstrate that they have progressed through an export-readiness and business-strengthening process.

Export accreditation may improve the credibility of a participating enterprise when engaging with:

  • Buyers;
  • Distributors;
  • Investors;
  • Financial institutions;
  • Government trade agencies;
  • Business-development organisations; and
  • Potential regional partners.

However, applicants should not interpret accreditation as an automatic export licence or a guarantee that the business will be admitted into every target market. Country-specific legal and regulatory requirements may still apply.

  1. Facilitated Intra-African Export Through Policy Sandboxes

One of the programme’s most distinctive components is the use of Policy Sandboxes.

The Policy Sandbox approach is intended to help qualified enterprises navigate and address non-tariff barriers that may prevent or delay cross-border trade.

Such barriers may include:

  • Complex customs procedures;
  • Different regulatory requirements;
  • Product certification problems;
  • Licensing complications;
  • Inconsistent standards;
  • Administrative delays;
  • Weak aggregation systems;
  • Market-access restrictions; and
  • Limited coordination between relevant institutions.

Through the Policy Sandboxes, programme participants may be able to engage with relevant public, private and regulatory stakeholders to identify practical solutions.

The approach goes beyond advising businesses about barriers. It seeks to create a structured environment in which enterprises can work through actual challenges affecting their export processes.

The Policy Sandbox pilot will initially focus on the six participating countries.

  1. Exposure to Continental Business and Financing Opportunities

The final stage of the accelerator involves connecting participating enterprises with broader business and financing opportunities across Africa.

This exposure may help businesses:

  • Identify potential buyers;
  • Connect with regional distributors;
  • Meet investors and financial institutions;
  • Build strategic partnerships;
  • Explore opportunities in regional value chains;
  • Increase their business visibility;
  • Enter new markets;
  • Mobilise growth capital; and
  • Develop long-term commercial relationships.

Exposure to financing opportunities should not be confused with guaranteed funding. Participants may still need to meet the requirements of individual investors, lenders, grant providers or financial institutions.

Complete E-Accelerator Journey

The programme consists of the following eight stages:

  1. Application and Diagnostics;
  2. Selection and Announcement of the Pilot Cohort;
  3. Export FitLab;
  4. Tailored Enterprise Advisory;
  5. Continental Bootcamp during the YouthConnekt Africa Summit;
  6. Business Export Accreditation;
  7. Facilitated Intra-African Export through Policy Sandboxes; and
  8. Exposure to Continental Business and Financing Opportunities.

Each stage contributes to the wider objective of helping established youth-led businesses become more prepared, competitive and capable of participating in regional trade.

Key Benefits of Participating

Selected entrepreneurs may benefit from:

  • A detailed assessment of their enterprise’s export readiness;
  • Identification of business weaknesses and growth barriers;
  • Tailored advisory services based on individual business needs;
  • Support in strengthening enterprise operations;
  • Improved understanding of export requirements;
  • Assistance in navigating customs and regulatory processes;
  • Support in addressing non-tariff barriers;
  • Preparation for entry into new African markets;
  • Participation in the Export FitLab;
  • Access to a continental business bootcamp;
  • Progress towards Business Export Accreditation;
  • Engagement through Policy Sandboxes;
  • Exposure to investors and financial institutions;
  • Connections with potential buyers and business partners;
  • Improved regional business networks;
  • Increased enterprise visibility;
  • Greater understanding of the AfCFTA market;
  • Opportunities to mobilise investment;
  • Potential for business expansion; and
  • Potential to create sustainable employment.

The precise support provided to each enterprise may depend on its diagnostic results, readiness level and identified growth needs.

Information Applicants Should Prepare

Because the official registration form also functions as a business diagnostic, applicants should prepare detailed and accurate information before beginning.

Relevant information may include:

Personal Information

  • Full name;
  • Age or date of birth;
  • Nationality;
  • Country of residence;
  • Contact information;
  • Current position in the enterprise; and
  • Ownership or leadership role.

Business Information

  • Business name;
  • Registration status;
  • Year of establishment;
  • Business location;
  • Sector or industry;
  • Products or services offered;
  • Business model;
  • Target customers;
  • Current markets;
  • Number of employees;
  • Production or service-delivery capacity; and
  • Current business operations.

Evidence of Business Progress

Applicants should be prepared to explain:

  • The customers they currently serve;
  • The level of demand for their offering;
  • Important partnerships;
  • Sales or revenue activity;
  • Business growth achieved;
  • Jobs created;
  • Distribution channels;
  • Product development progress; and
  • Any recognition, certification or previous business support received.

Export and Expansion Information

Applicants may need to describe:

  • Their preferred African target markets;
  • Reasons for selecting those markets;
  • Potential customers in those markets;
  • Existing regional demand;
  • Their market-entry strategy;
  • Export experience, where applicable;
  • Regulatory challenges;
  • Product-certification needs;
  • Customs or logistical barriers;
  • Distribution requirements;
  • Financing needs; and
  • Expected outcomes from the accelerator.

How to Prepare a Strong Application

Provide Complete and Accurate Information

The application form specifically encourages applicants to provide both mandatory and optional information.

Incomplete answers may prevent the organisers from properly assessing the business or understanding the type of support it requires.

Applicants should provide honest and verifiable information about:

  • Business operations;
  • Customers;
  • Sales;
  • Employees;
  • Production capacity;
  • Financial needs; and
  • Regional-expansion plans.

Demonstrate That the Business Is Established

Applicants should clearly show that their enterprise is already operational.

Useful evidence may include:

  • Existing customers;
  • Sales records;
  • Business registration;
  • Product samples;
  • Operational facilities;
  • Online business presence;
  • Distribution arrangements;
  • Contracts or purchase orders;
  • Partnerships; and
  • Employment records.

Explain the Regional Opportunity

Applicants should identify where and how the business intends to expand.

Rather than stating that the enterprise wants to enter “all African countries,” a stronger application would identify specific priority markets and explain:

  • Why those markets are relevant;
  • What demand exists;
  • Which customers will be targeted;
  • How the product or service will be distributed;
  • What regulatory requirements apply; and
  • What support is required.

Identify the Actual Business Barrier

Applicants should clearly describe what is currently preventing expansion.

Examples include:

  • Lack of market information;
  • Product-certification requirements;
  • Limited production capacity;
  • Customs procedures;
  • Logistics costs;
  • Weak distribution networks;
  • Packaging requirements;
  • Limited working capital;
  • Regulatory uncertainty;
  • Lack of export documentation; or
  • Difficulty finding reliable regional partners.

Describe Measurable Expected Outcomes

Applicants should explain what they hope to achieve through the accelerator.

Possible outcomes may include:

  • Entering one new African market;
  • Securing a regional distributor;
  • Completing a required certification;
  • Developing an export strategy;
  • Increasing production capacity;
  • Attracting growth investment;
  • Establishing cross-border sales;
  • Increasing employment; or
  • Building a regional partnership.

Measurable objectives are generally more convincing than vague statements about wanting to “grow the business.”

No Participation Fees

YouthConnekt Africa states that it does not charge fees for participation in its programmes.

Applicants should not pay individuals or organisations claiming that payment is required to:

  • Submit an application;
  • Secure selection;
  • Accelerate approval;
  • Arrange an interview;
  • Access programme officials; or
  • Guarantee participation.

The programme also states that YouthConnekt Africa staff and partners will not request payment or communicate with applicants through personal channels regarding programme applications.

Official communications are expected to be sent through recognised official email channels.

Applicants should verify the authenticity of any message received before sharing confidential business information or making any payment.

How to Apply

Interested entrepreneurs should complete the official Youth-Led Enterprise Registration and Diagnostic Form.

Applicants should follow these steps:

  1. Confirm that they are below 35 years of age.
  2. Confirm that they are from one of the six eligible pilot countries.
  3. Ensure that their business is already established locally.
  4. Assess whether the business is genuinely ready for regional expansion.
  5. Gather accurate personal and business information.
  6. Prepare evidence of business operations and progress.
  7. Identify preferred African target markets.
  8. Explain the barriers affecting expansion.
  9. Complete all mandatory application questions.
  10. Provide relevant optional information where possible.
  11. Review the application carefully.
  12. Submit the form before the deadline.

The application form is intentionally detailed because the submitted information will support the programme’s diagnostic process and help shape any tailored enterprise advisory offered to selected businesses. Click HERE to apply now.

Application Deadline

The application deadline for the YouthConnekt Africa Export Accelerator 2026 is 16 August 2026.

Eligible applicants are encouraged to submit their applications early rather than waiting until the final day.

Important Information Not Specified

The currently available programme information does not clearly state:

  • The total number of enterprises to be selected;
  • The full programme duration;
  • The date when the pilot cohort will be announced;
  • Whether the programme will be delivered physically, virtually or through a hybrid format;
  • Whether bootcamp travel expenses will be covered;
  • Whether accommodation will be provided;
  • Whether selected enterprises will receive direct grants;
  • Whether participants will receive stipends;
  • The amount of financing potentially available;
  • A restricted list of eligible business sectors; or
  • The detailed assessment and scoring criteria.

Applicants should not make assumptions about these matters. They should rely on subsequent official communications from YouthConnekt Africa.

Conclusion

The African Union–UNDP YouthConnekt Africa Export Accelerator 2026 presents an important opportunity for young entrepreneurs who have already built functioning businesses and are prepared to pursue regional growth.

Through business diagnostics, the Export FitLab, tailored enterprise advisory, export accreditation, Policy Sandboxes, continental networking and exposure to financing opportunities, the programme aims to help youth-led enterprises become more competitive and better prepared for intra-African trade.

The opportunity is particularly valuable for entrepreneurs who have demonstrated local business potential but require specialised support to understand export requirements, address regulatory constraints, identify suitable markets and establish regional commercial relationships.

Applicants should remember that the programme is not a grant-only opportunity. It is designed for ambitious entrepreneurs who are willing to strengthen their businesses and actively participate in the process of entering new African markets.

Eligible entrepreneurs below the age of 35 from Rwanda, Uganda, Senegal, The Gambia, Zambia and Zimbabwe should prepare comprehensive and accurate business information and submit the official registration and diagnostic form by 16 August 2026.

For more global opportunities, VISIT HERE.

 

The post African Union–UNDP YouthConnekt Africa Export Accelerator 2026: Applications Open for Youth-Led Businesses Expanding Across Africa appeared first on Opportunities for Youth.

item.title: Applications Open for the 2026 Education Cannot Wait Youth and Student-led Sub-group Membership and Election
item.dc:creator: Nana Safo
item.description:
Youth-led and student-led organizations working to advance education in emergencies and crisis-affected communities are invited to apply for membership in the Education Cannot Wait (ECW) Youth and Student-led Sub-group. The 2026 Membership and Election Call provides eligible organizations with an opportunity to join a global constituency that contributes to ECW’s governance and helps ensure that […]

The post Applications Open for the 2026 Education Cannot Wait Youth and Student-led Sub-group Membership and Election appeared first on Opportunities for Youth.
item.content:encoded:
Youth-led and student-led organizations working to advance education in emergencies and crisis-affected communities are invited to apply for membership in the Education Cannot Wait (ECW) Youth and Student-led Sub-group.

The 2026 Membership and Election Call provides eligible organizations with an opportunity to join a global constituency that contributes to ECW’s governance and helps ensure that the perspectives of young people and crisis-affected communities are reflected in decision-making.

Through this opportunity, eligible organizations can apply for membership in the ECW Youth and Student-led Sub-group. Eligible organizations may also stand for election to represent the constituency on key ECW governance bodies, including the High-Level Steering Group (HLSG) and the Executive Committee (ExCom), for a two-year mandate.

About Education Cannot Wait and the Youth and Student-led Sub-group

Education Cannot Wait, commonly known as ECW, is a global fund and partnership dedicated to ensuring that children and young people affected by crises and emergencies have access to quality education.

The ECW Youth and Student-led Sub-group provides a platform for youth-led and student-led organizations to contribute to discussions on education in emergencies, participate in consultations on ECW policies and priorities, and help ensure that the perspectives of young people and crisis-affected communities are reflected in global education decision-making.

Opportunities Available Under the 2026 Call

The 2026 Membership and Election Call offers eligible organizations two key opportunities: applying for membership and standing for election.

Apply for Membership

Eligible youth-led and student-led organizations can apply to become members of the Education Cannot Wait Youth and Student-led Sub-group.

Membership provides organizations with the opportunity to participate in a global constituency focused on youth participation and education in crisis settings.

Members may be able to:

  • Participate in consultations on ECW policies and priorities.
  • Contribute perspectives on issues affecting young people and crisis-affected communities.
  • Connect with organizations working in education in emergencies.
  • Engage with a global network of youth-led and student-led organizations.
  • Participate in elections for representatives of the constituency.
  • Contribute to discussions related to education priorities and governance.

Through membership, organizations can engage with global stakeholders and contribute their knowledge and perspectives to discussions concerning education in emergencies and crisis-affected communities.

Stand for Election

Eligible organizations may also stand for election to represent the Youth and Student-led Sub-group on key ECW governance bodies.

Representatives may be elected to serve on:

  • The High-Level Steering Group (HLSG).
  • The Executive Committee (ExCom).

Elected representatives will serve a two-year mandate.

This presents an opportunity for eligible youth-led and student-led organizations to take on a formal representative role and help ensure that the perspectives of young people and crisis-affected communities are considered in high-level discussions and decision-making.

Benefits of Participating

Participation in the ECW Youth and Student-led Sub-group can provide several important benefits to eligible organizations.

Contribute to Global Education Policy Discussions

Members can participate in consultations related to ECW policies and priorities. This allows organizations working directly with young people and communities to contribute their experiences and perspectives to global discussions.

Represent Young People and Crisis-Affected Communities

The opportunity provides a platform for organizations to help ensure that the voices of young people and crisis-affected communities are considered in global education decision-making.

This is particularly important in contexts affected by conflict, displacement, natural disasters, and other humanitarian emergencies.

Build Global Connections and Partnerships

Members can connect with other organizations working in education in emergencies and related areas.

These connections can support:

  • Knowledge exchange.
  • Partnership development.
  • Collaboration.
  • Sharing of experiences.
  • The development of effective approaches to education challenges.

Participate in Governance

In addition to networking and consultation opportunities, eligible members can participate in elections for representatives of the constituency.

Organizations that stand for election may have the opportunity to represent the Youth and Student-led Sub-group on the ECW High-Level Steering Group or Executive Committee.

Eligibility Requirements

The opportunity is intended for eligible organizations rather than individual applicants.

Organizations interested in applying should be:

  • Not-for-profit organizations.
  • Youth-led or student-led organizations.
  • Working in education, emergencies, or crisis contexts.
  • Eligible under the requirements set out in the official Membership & Election Guidance Note.

Organizations should carefully review the complete eligibility criteria before submitting a membership application or election nomination.

The Membership & Election Guidance Note provides additional information on eligibility requirements, the application process, the election process, member responsibilities, and other important requirements for participating organizations.

Countries and Regions Eligible to Apply

The opportunity is open to eligible organizations from around the world.

The information provided does not restrict applications to a specific country, race, or continent. However, organizations must meet the relevant eligibility requirements outlined in the official guidance documents.

Interested organizations should consult the full Membership & Election Guidance Note for detailed information on eligibility and organizational requirements.

Important Dates

The key dates for the 2026 ECW Youth and Student-led Sub-group Membership and Election Call are as follows:

  • Membership applications open: 6 July 2026.
  • Deadline for membership applications and election nominations: 26 July 2026.
  • Voting period: 3–5 August 2026.
  • Announcement of elected representatives: 7 August 2026.

Application Deadline

The deadline for membership applications and election nominations is 26 July 2026.

Organizations interested in participating are encouraged to begin preparing their applications early to ensure that they have sufficient time to review the eligibility requirements and submit all necessary information before the deadline.

How to Apply

Eligible organizations can apply through the appropriate application process.

Organizations seeking membership in the ECW Youth and Student-led Sub-group should complete the Membership Application Form HERE

Organizations wishing to stand for election to represent the constituency should complete the Election Nomination Form HERE

Applicants should also download and carefully review the Membership & Election Guidance Note before submitting their application or nomination.

The guidance note provides important information regarding:

  • Eligibility.
  • The membership process.
  • The election process.
  • Member responsibilities.
  • The role of elected representatives.

Election Process

Following the application and nomination period, voting is scheduled to take place from 3 to 5 August 2026.

The elected representatives are expected to be announced on 7 August 2026.

Organizations seeking to stand for election should ensure that they understand the election process and meet all relevant requirements before submitting their nominations.

Steps to Take Before Applying

Interested organizations should consider the following steps before applying:

  1. Review the eligibility requirements carefully and confirm that the organization qualifies to participate.
  2. Download and read the Membership & Election Guidance Note to understand the requirements, responsibilities, and election process.
  3. Determine whether the organization will apply for membership, stand for election, or pursue both opportunities where permitted.
  4. Prepare the required organizational information and supporting details before completing the relevant form.
  5. Complete the appropriate Membership Application Form or Election Nomination Form.
  6. Submit the application or nomination before the deadline of 26 July 2026.
  7. Contact the relevant representative if clarification is required.

Questions about the opportunity can be directed to Hector Ulloa, the current ExCom Representative for the ECW Youth and Student-led Sub-group, at hector@globalstudentforum.org.

Why This Opportunity Matters

Young people and students are among those most directly affected by education disruptions resulting from conflict, displacement, natural disasters, humanitarian emergencies, and other crises.

At the same time, youth-led and student-led organizations often have direct knowledge of the challenges affecting young people and communities in these contexts.

The ECW Youth and Student-led Sub-group provides an important platform for these perspectives to contribute to global discussions and governance.

By joining the sub-group or participating in the election process, eligible organizations can contribute to strengthening youth participation in education decision-making and help promote greater consideration of the needs and experiences of children and young people affected by crises.

For more membership opportunities, visit OFY Website HERE

Conclusion

The 2026 Education Cannot Wait Youth and Student-led Sub-group Membership and Election Call offers an important opportunity for eligible not-for-profit youth-led and student-led organizations working in education, emergencies, and crisis contexts.

Successful members can participate in consultations on ECW policies and priorities, connect with organizations working in education in emergencies, and participate in elections for representatives to the constituency’s governance bodies.

Eligible organizations may also stand for election to represent the Youth and Student-led Sub-group on the High-Level Steering Group and Executive Committee for a two-year mandate.

Organizations interested in participating should carefully review the official Membership & Election Guidance Note, complete the appropriate application or nomination form, and submit their materials before the deadline.

Deadline: 26 July 2026.

The post Applications Open for the 2026 Education Cannot Wait Youth and Student-led Sub-group Membership and Election appeared first on Opportunities for Youth.

item.title: MENA Junior Fellowship 2026: Apply for the Third Edition of the Three-Month Research Programme
item.dc:creator: Emmanuel Mensah
item.description:
Applications are open for the MENA Junior Fellowship 2026, a three-month research programme created for early-career scholars and independent researchers working on topics related to the Middle East and North Africa. Now entering its third edition, the fellowship aims to help emerging researchers strengthen their research skills, advance their academic development and connect with an […]

The post MENA Junior Fellowship 2026: Apply for the Third Edition of the Three-Month Research Programme appeared first on Opportunities for Youth.
item.content:encoded:
Applications are open for the MENA Junior Fellowship 2026, a three-month research programme created for early-career scholars and independent researchers working on topics related to the Middle East and North Africa.

Now entering its third edition, the fellowship aims to help emerging researchers strengthen their research skills, advance their academic development and connect with an international research network.

Selected fellows will receive research training, mentorship, academic supervision and publication support. Participants will also gain affiliation with a European-based research institute and access to an interdisciplinary research community.

The programme will begin on 1 September 2026. Applications must be submitted by 16 August 2026.

About the MENA Junior Fellowship 2026

The MENA Junior Fellowship is designed for researchers at the beginning of their academic or professional journeys who are conducting research on MENA-related topics.

The programme provides a structured environment in which participants can develop their research abilities while receiving guidance from experienced researchers. It also introduces fellows to an international community of scholars working across different disciplines.

The fellowship may be especially valuable for independent researchers and early-career scholars who want additional academic supervision, publication guidance or stronger connections with research institutions and scholarly networks.

Programme Duration and Start Date

The third edition of the MENA Junior Fellowship will run for three months.

The official programme start date is:

1 September 2026

Applicants should ensure that they are available to participate in the programme from the stated start date.

Who Should Apply?

The fellowship is intended for:

  • Early-career scholars
  • Independent researchers
  • Researchers working on MENA-related topics
  • Emerging researchers seeking to strengthen their academic development
  • Scholars interested in joining an international research network

Applicants should have a clear research interest connected to the Middle East and North Africa.

Main Fellowship Benefits

The updated announcement confirms that selected fellows will receive:

  • Research training
  • Mentorship
  • Academic supervision
  • Publication support
  • Affiliation with a European-based research institute
  • Access to an interdisciplinary research community
  • Connection to an international research network
  • Support for their academic development

How to Apply

Applicants should carefully review the application page for full information concerning:

  • Eligibility requirements
  • Required documents
  • Research proposal requirements
  • Selection procedures
  • Programme format
  • Attendance expectations
  • Language requirements
  • Institutional affiliation
  • Any possible costs or financial support

APPLY HERE

Important Dates

  • Application deadline: 16 August 2026

  • Programme start date: 1 September 2026

  • Programme duration: Three months

Conclusion

The MENA Junior Fellowship 2026 offers early-career scholars and independent researchers an opportunity to strengthen their research skills and connect with an international academic community.

Through research training, mentorship, academic supervision, publication support and affiliation with a European-based research institute, the programme is designed to support participants as they develop their academic trajectories.

Researchers working on topics related to the Middle East and North Africa should review the official application information and submit their applications before 16 August 2026.

The three-month programme begins on 1 September 2026.

For further information or inquiries, CLICK HERE.

To discover more fellowship opportunities, VISIT HERE.

The post MENA Junior Fellowship 2026: Apply for the Third Edition of the Three-Month Research Programme appeared first on Opportunities for Youth.

item.title: GEF SGP Southern Africa Regional Workshop 2026: Apply for a Strategic Grant of Up to USD 100,000
item.dc:creator: Ning Ma
item.description:
The Global Environment Facility Small Grants Programme, implemented by the United Nations Development Programme, is inviting eligible organisations to submit proposals to host and deliver the GEF SGP Southern Africa Regional Workshop 2026. The regional workshop will take place in Harare, Zimbabwe, from 21 to 25 September 2026 and will bring together approximately 40 participants […]

The post GEF SGP Southern Africa Regional Workshop 2026: Apply for a Strategic Grant of Up to USD 100,000 appeared first on Opportunities for Youth.
item.content:encoded:
The Global Environment Facility Small Grants Programme, implemented by the United Nations Development Programme, is inviting eligible organisations to submit proposals to host and deliver the GEF SGP Southern Africa Regional Workshop 2026.

The regional workshop will take place in Harare, Zimbabwe, from 21 to 25 September 2026 and will bring together approximately 40 participants connected to the Small Grants Programme across Southern Africa and the wider GEF SGP network.

The selected organisation will be responsible for coordinating the workshop, participant travel, accommodation, conferencing, field visits, exhibitions, visibility materials and documentation. The maximum proposed budget is USD 100,000, and co-financing in cash or in kind is mandatory.

Applications must be submitted by 27 July 2026 at 23:59 Central Africa Time.

About the GEF Small Grants Programme

Established in 1992, the GEF Small Grants Programme supports community-led initiatives addressing global environmental and sustainable development challenges.

The programme focuses on areas including:

  • Biodiversity conservation
  • Climate change mitigation and adaptation
  • Land degradation and sustainable land management
  • International waters
  • Chemicals and waste management

The Southern Africa Regional Workshop will create a platform for country representatives to review progress under Operational Phase 7, share lessons and good practices, discuss implementation challenges and strengthen cooperation as countries move forward with Operational Phase 8.

Workshop Participants and Countries

The workshop is expected to host 40 participants.

Represented Southern African countries include:

  • Botswana
  • Eswatini
  • Lesotho
  • Malawi
  • Mauritius
  • Mozambique
  • Namibia
  • Seychelles
  • South Africa
  • Zambia
  • Zimbabwe

Additional regional and global GEF SGP representatives may also attend.

Main Objectives of the Workshop

The workshop will enable National Coordinators and other participants to:

  1. Review progress and achievements under Operational Phase 7.
  2. Examine results, challenges and lessons from country and project-level implementation.
  3. Identify effective landscape approaches and good practices.
  4. Strengthen peer learning through project site visits.
  5. Support regional collaboration during the transition to Operational Phase 8.
  6. Produce regional knowledge materials documenting results, impacts and lessons from Southern Africa.

Responsibilities of the Selected Organisation

The successful applicant will organise and manage several major components.

Participant Travel and Allowances

The organisation will arrange return economy air tickets and applicable daily subsistence allowances for participants travelling from outside Zimbabwe.

Accommodation and Conference Facilities

Applicants must obtain at least three hotel quotations in Harare during proposal development.

The selected organisation will be expected to:

  • Book a suitable venue
  • Arrange accommodation
  • Coordinate participant check-in
  • Prepare logistical and information materials
  • Provide meals and refreshments where required
  • Support workshop facilitation and daily logistics

Project Site Visits and Exhibitions

The programme includes visits to selected GEF SGP-supported projects in Harare.

The organisation will arrange transport for participants, coordinate exhibitions and provide catering and logistical support for participating grantees.

Visibility and Information Materials

Required materials may include branded clothing, diaries, brochures, banners, gazebos, exhibition furniture and a workshop backdrop.

Documentation

The organisation must produce:

  • A workshop report
  • A video documentary covering the workshop and site visits
  • A Southern Africa publication presenting projects, OP7 results, impacts and lessons learned

The call places substantial emphasis on workshop documentation and the production of knowledge materials.

Grant Amount and Project Duration

The proposal budget must:

  • Be prepared in United States dollars
  • Not exceed USD 100,000
  • Include detailed projected activities and expenses
  • Include mandatory co-financing in cash, in kind or both

The grant period is six months, and the project must be completed by 31 December 2026.

Applicants should use the supplied GEF SGP proposal and budget templates. Although the standard generic template contains broader SGP guidance, applicants should follow the specific financial ceiling and conditions stated in this call.

Eligible Organisations

Eligible applicants must be registered organisations with the capacity to organise international workshops and produce professional knowledge products.

Eligible entities include:

  • Civil society organisations
  • National non-governmental organisations
  • Non-governmental academic institutions
  • Non-governmental educational institutions

Applicants must provide proof of registration or evidence that Private Voluntary Organisation registration is underway.

The following are not eligible:

  • Individuals
  • Private-sector companies
  • Commercial entities
  • National government institutions
  • Regional government institutions
  • Local government institutions

The applicant must demonstrate relevant experience, strong organisational capacity and a proven record aligned with the assignment.

Required Personnel and Experience

Applications must include CVs for three key personnel:

  • A project manager with at least three years of project management experience
  • A lead finance officer with at least three years of finance experience
  • A monitoring and evaluation officer with at least three years of relevant experience

Applicants should also demonstrate experience in:

  • Organising international workshops
  • Providing logistical support
  • Facilitating workshops
  • Producing publications, videos and reports
  • Managing knowledge products
  • Working on relevant GEF SGP environmental themes

Required Application Documents

Applicants must submit:

  • A completed GEF SGP proposal template
  • A completed budget template
  • Proof of organisational registration
  • At least three partner or referee support letters
  • CVs of key project personnel
  • Evidence of previous publications, videos or workshop reports
  • A list of board members with certified identification copies
  • Signed minutes from the last two board meetings

Audited financial statements are considered an advantage.

Only one submission per organisation is allowed, and hard-copy applications will not be accepted. The complete application must be submitted electronically using the email address stated in the call documents.

Application Deadline

The application deadline is:

27 July 2026 at 23:59 CAT

Late proposals will not be considered. Only successful applicants will be contacted.

Conclusion

The GEF SGP Southern Africa Regional Workshop strategic grant offers an important opportunity for an experienced civil society, academic or educational organisation to manage a major regional environmental event in Harare.

The successful organisation will coordinate travel, accommodation, workshop delivery, field visits, exhibitions and professional documentation for a regional gathering of 40 participants. Applicants must demonstrate strong event-management experience, institutional capacity, environmental knowledge and the ability to deliver the assignment within a budget not exceeding USD 100,000.

Eligible organisations should complete the required proposal and budget templates and submit all supporting documents before 27 July 2026 at 23:59 CAT.

For further information and application requirements, CLICK HERE.

To download the proposal template, CLICK HERE.

To discover more grants opportunities, VISIT HERE.

The post GEF SGP Southern Africa Regional Workshop 2026: Apply for a Strategic Grant of Up to USD 100,000 appeared first on Opportunities for Youth.

item.title: Doha Debates Ambassador Program Cohort 9 (2026): Global Leadership Opportunity for Young Changemakers Aged 18–26
item.dc:creator: Isaac O
item.description:
Published Date: 17 July 2026 Applications are now open for Cohort 9 of the Doha Debates Ambassador Program, a prestigious 12-week virtual leadership programme designed for young people who are passionate about improving public discourse, strengthening intercultural understanding, and driving positive change in their communities and beyond. Hosted by Doha Debates, the programme brings together […]

The post Doha Debates Ambassador Program Cohort 9 (2026): Global Leadership Opportunity for Young Changemakers Aged 18–26 appeared first on Opportunities for Youth.
item.content:encoded:
Published Date: 17 July 2026

Applications are now open for Cohort 9 of the Doha Debates Ambassador Program, a prestigious 12-week virtual leadership programme designed for young people who are passionate about improving public discourse, strengthening intercultural understanding, and driving positive change in their communities and beyond.

Hosted by Doha Debates, the programme brings together emerging leaders from around the world to participate in expert-led training, collaborative learning, mentorship, and meaningful dialogue on complex global issues. Participants develop practical leadership, communication, storytelling, systems thinking, and debate skills while building lifelong relationships with an international network of peers.

The programme is entirely virtual, making it accessible to talented young leaders worldwide who meet the eligibility requirements.

The application deadline is 24 August 2026.

About the Doha Debates Ambassador Program

The Doha Debates Ambassador Program is a unique international leadership initiative that empowers young people to become more effective communicators, bridge cultural divides, and facilitate productive conversations around today’s most pressing global challenges.

Rather than focusing solely on debate, the programme encourages participants to cultivate empathy, understand diverse perspectives, and learn how to engage in respectful dialogue across cultures and viewpoints.

Over a period of 12 weeks, selected ambassadors participate in structured learning experiences that combine:

  • Weekly live virtual sessions
  • Interactive workshops
  • Expert-led training
  • Mentorship
  • Collaborative group work
  • Personal storytelling
  • Cross-cultural dialogue
  • Practical leadership development

The programme concludes with a global virtual dialogue event known as the World Majlis, where ambassadors apply everything they have learned throughout the programme.

Why the Doha Debates Ambassador Program Was Created

Today’s global challenges require cooperation across cultures, disciplines, and societies.

Doha Debates believes that young people are essential partners in creating a more peaceful, inclusive, and collaborative world. However, meaningful collaboration requires spaces where individuals can openly exchange ideas while respecting differing opinions.

The Ambassador Program draws inspiration from the traditional Majlis, a gathering place where important issues are discussed openly and respectfully.

Within this modern virtual Majlis, participants:

  • Build trust across cultures.
  • Strengthen intercultural understanding.
  • Develop collaborative leadership skills.
  • Learn to facilitate difficult conversations.
  • Explore diverse perspectives.
  • Transform dialogue into meaningful action.

What Participants Will Learn

Throughout the programme, ambassadors receive training in a wide range of practical leadership and communication skills.

Participants will learn how to:

  • Facilitate discussions on complex global issues.
  • Participate confidently in challenging conversations.
  • Navigate intercultural communication effectively.
  • Understand diverse worldviews.
  • Clearly articulate their own perspectives.
  • Use storytelling to inspire and motivate others.
  • Build empathy across cultural differences.
  • Develop systems thinking approaches to solving global problems.
  • Foster inclusive dialogue within their own communities.
  • Engage respectfully with people holding different viewpoints.

Skills You Will Gain

By participating in the Doha Debates Ambassador Program, participants strengthen valuable lifelong skills, including:

  • Public speaking
  • Debate and dialogue
  • Active listening
  • Cross-cultural communication
  • Leadership
  • Critical thinking
  • Collaboration
  • Community engagement
  • Storytelling
  • Facilitation
  • Systems thinking
  • Global citizenship

These skills are valuable for careers in international development, diplomacy, public policy, education, advocacy, entrepreneurship, civil society, and many other fields.

Program Structure

The programme is conducted entirely online over a 12-week period.

Participants should expect to dedicate approximately:

4–5 hours per week

Activities include:

Weekly Live Sessions

  • 90-minute Zoom meetings every week.

Online Learning

Participants engage asynchronously through the Doha Debates Learning Community.

Mentorship

Participants interact with experienced mentors and facilitators throughout the programme.

Collaborative Projects

Ambassadors work together with peers from around the world.

Final World Majlis

The programme concludes with a special two-hour World Majlis, during which participants facilitate virtual dialogues using the skills they have developed.

Program Timeline

Applicants should take note of the following important dates.

Activity Date
Applications Open 1 July 2026
Application Deadline 24 August 2026
Candidate Interviews 8–11 September 2026
Acceptance Notifications 15 September 2026
Ambassador Program 22 September – 1 December 2026
World Majlis Event 8 December 2026

Benefits of the Program

Participants selected for Cohort 9 will receive numerous professional and personal development opportunities.

Benefits include:

  • Participation in a prestigious international youth leadership programme.
  • Expert-led workshops and training sessions.
  • Mentorship from experienced facilitators.
  • Development of leadership and communication skills.
  • Practical experience facilitating dialogue.
  • Cross-cultural learning experiences.
  • Networking with young leaders from around the world.
  • Access to the Doha Debates global community.
  • Opportunities to participate in future Doha Debates events.
  • Eligibility to become a mentor for future cohorts.
  • Official Certificate of Completion upon successful participation.

Who Can Apply?

The programme welcomes young leaders from around the world.

Applicants must satisfy all eligibility requirements.

Candidates must:

  • Complete the full three-step application process.
  • Be 18–26 years old when the programme begins on 22 September 2026.
  • Have reliable internet access suitable for video conferencing.
  • Be fluent in English.
  • Be available every Tuesday at 6:00 p.m. AST throughout the programme from 22 September to 1 December 2026.

How to Apply

Applications consist of three mandatory stages.

Step 1: Complete the Introduction to the Majlis Course

Applicants must first complete the Introduction to the Majlis Course within the Doha Debates Learning Community.

Successful completion earns applicants the required Majlis Master Badge.

Important:

Applicants who completed the course in previous years must complete it again for Cohort 9.

Step 2: Submit the Online Application

After earning the badge, applicants receive:

  • Official badge confirmation
  • Link to the application form

The application requires:

  • Basic personal information
  • A personal video introducing yourself
  • Reflection on the Introduction to the Majlis course

Applications cannot be submitted until the course has been completed.

Step 3: Virtual Group Interview

Shortlisted finalists will be invited to participate in a virtual group interview between:

8–11 September 2026

Successful applicants will receive acceptance notifications by:

15 September 2026

Certificate

Participants who successfully complete all programme requirements will receive an official Doha Debates Certificate.

APPLY HERE

Alumni Opportunities

Graduates of the Ambassador Program remain connected to the Doha Debates community.

Alumni may:

  • Attend future events.
  • Participate in workshops.
  • Join discussions and debates.
  • Build long-term professional networks.
  • Be invited to mentor future programme participants.

Application Deadline

The deadline for applications is:

24 August 2026

Applicants should begin the process early because completing the required Introduction to the Majlis Course is mandatory before accessing the application form.

Visit HERE to learn more about the Doha Debates Ambassador Program Cohort 9

Conclusion

The Doha Debates Ambassador Program Cohort 9 provides an outstanding opportunity for young leaders who are passionate about dialogue, leadership, intercultural communication, and global collaboration.
